structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective EHR data from a single hospital system spanning 60 months (Jan 2019–Dec 2023). Data includes clinical notes, demographics, diagnoses (ICD-10), procedures (CPT), medications, vital signs, lab results, discharge summaries, and readmission status within 30 days of discharge. Approximately 50,000 patient encounters. Data extracted via institutional query with audit trail; completeness and data quality assessed per variable.
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary outcome (readmission): binary indicator of any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of index discharge. Secondary outcome (treatment efficacy): composite binary indicator based on documented clinical improvement metrics post-discharge (e.g., resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status improvement, or absence of adverse events). Efficacy label is derived from structured clinical assessments and progress notes within 14 days post-discharge; missing efficacy labels handled via sensitivity analysis (see validation approach).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with patient-level stratification. Data ordered by index discharge date. Training set: discharges from Jan 2019–Aug 2022 (80% of timeline, ~40,000 encounters). Validation set: discharges from Sept 2022–Dec 2022 (10% of timeline, ~5,000 encounters). Test set: discharges from Jan 2023–Dec 2023 (10% of timeline, ~5,000 encounters). Within each split, stratify by readmission outcome (yes/no) to preserve class distribution and ensure each split contains representative readmission rates. Rationale: Temporal split prevents future information leakage (e.g., readmission outcomes from 2023 would not be known during training on 2019–2022 data); stratification ensures class imbalance is consistent across splits and reflects real-world prevalence; patient-level split ensures no patient appears in multiple sets.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (imputation strategies, scaling, categorical encoders, feature selection) are fit exclusively on the training set. Fitted transformers are then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage from validation/test statistics influencing training. Specific stages: (1) Fit imputation (median for continuous, mode for categorical) on train; apply to val/test. (2) Fit StandardScaler on train continuous features; apply to val/test. (3) Fit one-hot encoder on train categorical features; apply to val/test. (4) Fit feature selection (if used) on train; apply to val/test.
structured_choices.features: Raw signals extracted from EHR: (1) Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance type. (2) Clinical: primary/secondary diagnoses (ICD-10 counts and Elixhauser comorbidity index), acute vs. chronic disease markers, lab abnormalities (glucose, creatinine, hemoglobin), vital sign derangements at admission/discharge. (3) Treatment history: prior readmissions (count in past 12 months), length of stay, discharge disposition, medications prescribed at discharge (drug classes, polypharmacy count). (4) Care process: number of ED visits pre-admission (12 months prior), specialist involvement, post-discharge follow-up scheduling status. (5) Temporal: seasonality (discharge month), day of week. Clinical notes (discharge summaries) are processed via TF-IDF vectorization fit on training set only. No derived features encode the label or future information (e.g., we do not include readmission date or post-discharge complications observed after the efficacy labeling window).
structured_choices.model: Multi-task learning model: a neural network with shared hidden layers (512 → 256 units, ReLU activations, batch normalization, dropout p=0.3) feeding into two task-specific output heads. Head 1 (readmission): single sigmoid output predicting 30-day readmission probability. Head 2 (efficacy): single sigmoid output predicting treatment efficacy. Joint loss: weighted sum of binary cross-entropy for each task (weights tuned on validation set, tested grid: {0.5:0.5, 0.6:0.4, 0.7:0.3}). The multi-task structure is motivated by the hypothesis that learning shared representations from both tasks improves generalization and efficiency (fewer parameters, shared feature learning) compared to separate single-task models.
structured_choices.baseline: Single-task model trained on the identical feature set and using the same architecture (shared layers → single readmission output head, no efficacy head). This baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over the same hyperparameter space (learning rate, batch size, dropout rate, hidden layer sizes, L2 regularization). Both models use the same preprocessing pipeline, same train/val/test split, and identical training procedures (Adam optimizer, early stopping on validation loss). The baseline is fair because it differs only in the presence of the auxiliary task (efficacy prediction), isolating the effect of multi-task learning. Both models are trained for a maximum of 100 epochs with early stopping (patience=5) if validation loss does not improve.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) for the readmission prediction task, evaluated on the held-out test set. AUROC is chosen because: (1) It is threshold-agnostic and reflects discriminative ability across all decision thresholds, reducing dependence on an arbitrary threshold selection. (2) Readmission is a clinical outcome with asymmetric costs—false negatives (missed high-risk patients) are more costly than false positives (flagging low-risk patients for review). AUROC captures the tradeoff without requiring pre-specified class weights. (3) AUROC is robust to class imbalance and does not assume equal misclassification costs. (4) It aligns with how clinicians will use the model: a calibrated risk score informs clinical judgment rather than a hard classification rule.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC (AUPRC) for readmission: captures performance in the presence of class imbalance and is sensitive to false positives, which matter for clinical workflows.
Sensitivity (recall) and specificity at clinically relevant decision points (e.g., threshold set to capture 90% of readmissions).
Brier score for readmission: measures calibration quality; important because clinicians rely on risk scores to make personalized decisions.
Efficacy prediction AUROC: to confirm the auxiliary task was learned meaningfully.
Model efficiency metric: parameters count and training time for multi-task vs. single-task model, to quantify computational efficiency gains.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (Sept–Dec 2022). A grid search over learning rate ∈ {0.0001, 0.001, 0.01}, batch size ∈ {16, 32, 64}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.5}, and task weight combinations (for multi-task model) is conducted. For each configuration, the model is trained on the training set, and validation loss and AUROC are monitored on the validation set. The configuration maximizing validation AUROC is selected. The test set is not used during this process. Early stopping is implemented: training stops if validation loss does not improve for 5 consecutive epochs, further reducing overfitting risk.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (Jan–Dec 2023 discharges) is used exactly once: after model selection is complete. Both the multi-task model (using the best hyperparameters from validation) and the baseline single-task model (using its own best hyperparameters from the same validation procedure) are evaluated on the test set using the primary metric (AUROC) and secondary metrics. No test set data is used for training, validation, or hyperparameter tuning. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (computed via bootstrap, 2000 resamples) to quantify uncertainty. No further tuning or model selection is performed after test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: Variability in post-discharge follow-up procedures and provider practices may influence readmission rates independent of model predictions. This is partially addressed by: (1) Including post-discharge follow-up scheduling status as a model feature, allowing the model to learn its association with readmission. (2) Stratified evaluation: stratify test set by follow-up status (scheduled vs. not scheduled) and report AUROC separately for each stratum to detect whether model performance is confounded by follow-up intensity. (3) Demographic fairness check: stratify test set evaluation by age groups, race/ethnicity, and insurance type. Report AUROC and calibration metrics (Brier score) for each group to identify potential demographic bias introduced by confounding or data quality differences. If performance or calibration differs significantly across groups, this indicates uncontrolled confounds requiring further investigation. (4) Temporal sensitivity: compare performance on earlier vs. later test months to detect drift in underlying readmission drivers (e.g., if post-discharge protocols changed mid-2023). (5) Propensity matching (optional, post-hoc): if a confounder (e.g., follow-up intensity) is associated with both the model's prediction and the outcome, a sensitivity analysis can employ inverse probability weighting to estimate the causal effect of predicted risk on readmission, separating confounding from true signal.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Task Learning for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Hypothesis and Objective
We seek to test whether a multi-task learning (MTL) model that jointly predicts 30-day readmission and post-discharge treatment efficacy is more efficient than a single-task baseline model that predicts readmission alone. "More efficient" is operationalized as higher discriminative performance (AUROC) with lower model complexity (parameter count, training time), suggesting that auxiliary task learning improves signal extraction without computational overhead.

### 2. Data Source and Labeling
We use five years of retrospective EHR data (2019–2023) from a single hospital system, encompassing ~50,000 patient encounters. Each record represents a unique hospital discharge and includes demographics, diagnoses, procedures, medications, vitals, labs, clinical notes, and a binary readmission outcome (any unplanned readmission within 30 days). The efficacy label is a composite binary indicator derived from clinical assessments within 14 days post-discharge (e.g., resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status). Missing efficacy labels are retained with sensitivity analysis to confirm robustness. All data extraction is audited for completeness; known quality issues (e.g., unstructured note variability) are documented and used to inform feature extraction decisions.

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy
To prevent temporal leakage, we employ a chronological split: training set (Jan 2019–Aug 2022, 80%), validation set (Sept–Dec 2022, 10%), test set (Jan–Dec 2023, 10%). Within each split, we stratify by readmission outcome to ensure class distribution is preserved, addressing class imbalance by design. This temporal strategy reflects the deployment scenario: the model is trained on historical data and evaluated on prospective data, mimicking real-world performance.

### 4. Features and Preprocessing
Features span five domains: (1) demographics (age, sex, race, insurance), (2) clinical (ICD-10 comorbidity indices, lab abnormalities, vital derangements), (3) treatment history (prior readmissions, length of stay, discharge disposition, medications), (4) care process (ED visits, specialist involvement, follow-up scheduling), (5) temporal (discharge month, day-of-week seasonality). Clinical notes are vectorized via TF-IDF. All preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing data leakage. Feature selection, if performed, is fit on train only.

### 5. Models and Baseline
The multi-task model is a neural network with shared hidden layers (512 → 256 units, ReLU, batch norm, dropout p=0.3) feeding into two task-specific heads: one for readmission (sigmoid), one for efficacy (sigmoid). The loss is a weighted sum of binary cross-entropies. The baseline is an identical architecture with a single readmission head and no efficacy task. Both receive equal hyperparameter tuning via grid search over learning rate, batch size, dropout, and (for MTL) task weights. This ensures the comparison isolates the effect of multi-task learning.

### 6. Primary Evaluation Metric and Rationale
We use AUROC for readmission prediction as the primary metric. AUROC is appropriate because: (1) It is threshold-agnostic and robust to class imbalance, (2) False negatives (missed high-risk patients) are more costly than false positives, and AUROC captures this asymmetry without arbitrary thresholding, (3) It directly reflects the model's discriminative ability—the core claim in the hypothesis. Secondary metrics include AUPRC, sensitivity/specificity at clinical thresholds, Brier score (calibration), and efficacy AUROC.

### 7. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (Sept–Dec 2022) via grid search. For each configuration, we train on the training set and monitor validation AUROC. The best configuration is selected and applied to both multi-task and baseline models. Early stopping (patience=5) is employed during training. The test set is untouched until final evaluation.

### 8. Confound Management and Sensitivity Checks
Post-discharge follow-up variability is a known confound. We address it by: (1) including follow-up status as a feature, (2) reporting stratified AUROC by follow-up status on the test set, (3) conducting demographic fairness checks (age, race, insurance) to detect bias, (4) examining temporal drift within the test period. If stratified performance diverges significantly, we employ inverse probability weighting to isolate true predictive signal from confounding.

### 9. Scope of Claims
This design establishes whether MTL improves readmission prediction efficiency within a single hospital system using retrospective data. It does not establish causality (confounders remain), generalizability to other hospitals (site-specific effects exist), or clinical utility (which requires prospective validation and decision impact analysis). The experiment is limited to comparing discriminative performance and computational efficiency.
